text: Dehydration of 2% or greater body mass is associated with impaired thermoregulatory function, elevated cardiovascular strain, and impaired aerobic exercise performance, particularly in warmer, longer, and more intense conditions.
Planned drinking is optimal for activities longer than 90 minutes, particularly in the heat, during higher-intensity exercise with high sweat rates, and when performance is a concern.
Drinking to thirst may be sufficient for short-duration exercise under 60-90 minutes, exercise in cooler conditions, and lower-intensity exercise.

Athletes with high sweat rates should determine their individual sweat rates under race-similar conditions (intensity, pace, environment) and tailor drinking to prevent body mass losses greater than 2%.
In humans, thirst is typically alleviated before complete rehydration is achieved, meaning ad libitum drinking often results in cumulative body water deficit when sweat rates are high.
When carbohydrate intake of 1 g/min is desired during exercise, planned drinking rather than drinking to thirst is recommended.
Athletes should never drink so much during exercise that body weight is gained, as this indicates overhydration.
